caveats: a: The occupational mix combines 611410, 611420, and 611430.
a: Anthropic data reflect selected product use and not all technically feasible or economically attractive automation.
a: Independent facilitators and coaches may be underrepresented in payroll data.
rho: No representative 611430 implementation study was found.
rho: Providers selling proprietary methodology may restrict model use even when tasks are technically exposed.
e: The supplied target-band count of 578 is margin-bridged and marked ESTIMATE.
e: The NAICS definition allows both direct-to-individual and employer programs and does not measure recurrence.
s5: No observed 611430 control-transfer rate or owner-age distribution was found.
s5: Reported transactions likely overrepresent larger, branded, and technology-enabled firms.

q: Retention should be higher for differentiated, embedded programs and lower for generic seminar catalogs.
d5: The industry projection combines three six-digit training industries.
d5: Employment and occupational openings are not external training-service quantity.
d5: Management-development budgets are economically cyclical even if long-run skill needs grow.

o: Operator necessity varies sharply between generic compliance content and leadership, coaching, or team interventions.
o: An online program may still have an accountable operator, so modality alone does not establish elimination.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Employer-funded repeat cohorts, annual programs, retainers, managed curricula, subscriptions with facilitation, or fixed-fee customized engagements; providers are paid for needs assessment, content, instruction, facilitation, administration, and outcome reporting.
lens.excluded_activities: Direct-to-consumer one-off seminars without repeat organizational customers, degree programs, advice-only human-resources consulting without training, captive internal academies, pure software or content licensing without accountable service, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: Privately owned firms providing recurring or repeat outsourced professional, leadership, management, compliance, communication, sales, and career-development training to external employers, including customized classroom, virtual, blended, simulation, cohort, and managed-program delivery.

a.bridge: BLS measures occupations for combined private NAICS 611400 rather than 611430, and Anthropic observes Claude task coverage rather than technically substitutable labor. I map the measured training-specialist, business-operations, management, instructional, sales, and administrative work to needs-analysis drafts, course authoring, exercises, assessments, learner communications, proposals, scheduling, and reporting. Computer training in the broader industry may bias exposure upward, while the management-training emphasis on human facilitation may bias it downward.
caveats: The occupational mix combines 611410, 611420, and 611430.
Anthropic data reflect selected product use and not all technically feasible or economically attractive automation.
Independent facilitators and coaches may be underrepresented in payroll data.
a.change_evidence: A 611430-only wage and contractor census mapped to detailed O*NET tasks, combined with time studies from at least 20 target-sized providers that distinguish already-automated LMS work from current manual work.
a.rationale: The broader private industry has 29.2% of employment in business and financial operations, including 14.5% in training and development specialists, plus 16.8% management and 14.3% instruction. AI can assist a large portion of research, drafting, personalization, assessment, feedback, proposal, and administration work, but live facilitation, organizational diagnosis, sensitive coaching, group dynamics, relationship selling, and accountable judgment remain less exposed.

d5.bridge: BLS projects employment in combined private NAICS 611400 and in training-and-development and management occupations, not constant-price 611430 service quantity. I use 9.9% projected 2024-34 employment growth for the broader private industry, 11% for training and development specialists, and 6.1% for management occupations as directional anchors, converting the decade rates to a moderate five-year increase. This can overstate external-provider demand if employers insource or self-serve, and understate it if technology, compliance, and organizational change increase retraining frequency.
caveats: The industry projection combines three six-digit training industries.
Employment and occupational openings are not external training-service quantity.
Management-development budgets are economically cyclical even if long-run skill needs grow.
d5.change_evidence: Constant-price employer spend, learner-seat, and training-hour data specific to external professional and management training, separated from internal L&D, events, consulting, and software through 2031.
d5.rationale: BLS expects continuous upskilling and compliance training to support training-specialist demand, while management employment also grows. Leadership transitions, hybrid work, technology adoption, and policy change sustain repeat needs, but budget cyclicality and self-service content constrain growth in the current service basket.

caveats: The supplied target-band count of 578 is margin-bridged and marked ESTIMATE.
The NAICS definition allows both direct-to-individual and employer programs and does not measure recurrence.
e.change_evidence: Firm-level classification of the supplied target-band universe using employer versus consumer revenue, repeat-revenue cohorts, service mix, customer concentration, facilitator dependence, ownership, and normalized EBITDA.
e.rationale: Employer-funded management and professional development often recurs as leaders are promoted, policies and strategies change, and new cohorts enter. Eligibility is reduced for consumer seminar businesses, keynote-led event models, advice-only consulting, pure content licensing, and founder-centric practices without transferable delivery systems.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 611430 ($5,489,761,947) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 611430 ($13,713,784,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1657 (margins.json[61]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Education EBITDA/Sales 16.57%); revenue/employee $235,944 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 611430); class <5: 5,845 firms, avg revenue/firm $364,602 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $60,415 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 794 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,460,458 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $241,998 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 461 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,915,232 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $483,054 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 403 firms, avg revenue/firm $7,418,285 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,229,210 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 94 firms, avg revenue/firm $34,405,351 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $5,700,967 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 81 firms, avg revenue/firm $35,252,926 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $5,841,410 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 578

o.bridge: BLS describes training-specialist duties and delivery modes across employers, not the share of 611430 demand requiring an external accountable operator. I treat needs assessment, customization, facilitation, evaluation, and extensive work with people as operator-requiring, while self-guided manuals and online applications demonstrate substitution potential. This may overstate operator need for generic knowledge transfer and understate it where coaching, group behavior, or client accountability dominates.
caveats: The BLS occupation spans internal and external training functions.
Operator necessity varies sharply between generic compliance content and leadership, coaching, or team interventions.
An online program may still have an accountable operator, so modality alone does not establish elimination.
o.change_evidence: Employer procurement and learner-outcome panels comparing external facilitated programs, internal delivery, self-paced platforms, and AI coaches, with renewal, behavior-change, and promotion outcomes by use case.
o.rationale: AI and self-paced libraries can supply explanations, scenarios, practice, and basic feedback. Much professional and management development still depends on diagnosing organizational context, convening groups, handling sensitive interpersonal dynamics, facilitating practice, adapting in real time, and credibly reporting outcomes to the employer.

caveats: No representative contract dataset was available.
Retention should be higher for differentiated, embedded programs and lower for generic seminar catalogs.
q.change_evidence: Customer-level contract and renewal data over five years showing pricing units, discounts, delivery hours, scope, facilitator mix, gross margin, churn, and AI-related procurement clauses.
q.rationale: Fixed-fee customized programs, cohorts, and retainers can retain labor savings because customers buy facilitation, relevance, and outcomes rather than hours alone. Competitive bids, renewal negotiations, internal L&D alternatives, and digital content comparisons will share part of visible savings, often through expanded customization or service levels instead of explicit price cuts.

caveats: No representative 611430 implementation study was found.
Providers selling proprietary methodology may restrict model use even when tasks are technically exposed.
rho.change_evidence: Audited before-and-after workflow data from target-sized providers showing labor hours, defects, client approvals, learner outcomes, facilitator adoption, gross margin, and renewals for each AI-enabled process.
rho.rationale: Content, assessment, communications, and administration workflows are digital and accessible to commodity AI, and virtual-learning infrastructure lowers integration barriers. Implementation is limited by client confidentiality, intellectual-property controls, hallucination and bias risk, facilitator adoption, bespoke methodology, outcome measurement, and buyers' expectation of credible human-led experiences.

caveats: No observed 611430 control-transfer rate or owner-age distribution was found.
Reported transactions likely overrepresent larger, branded, and technology-enabled firms.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of closed 611430 transactions from PitchBook, Capital IQ, DealStats, BizBuySell, and state ownership records, matched to a complete eligible-firm denominator and coded for control, survival, founder dependence, and buyer type.
s5.rationale: A sizeable estimated target universe and strategic adjacency to human-resources consulting, learning technology, staffing, and larger training providers create plausible transfer routes. Founder brands, personal client relationships, contractor-heavy delivery, and nontransferable intellectual property can instead produce earn-outs, acqui-hires, or closures that do not constitute durable qualifying transfers.

principal_driver: Repeat employer upskilling needs combine with highly digitizable content and program-administration work.
principal_weakness: Generic professional-development content is easy to self-serve, while differentiated firms may depend heavily on founders, facilitators, and client relationships.
